name: MAP — Modular Agentic Planner (2025, Nature Communications)
origin_domain: AI research (cognitive neuroscience-inspired LLM architectures, automated planning)
year_developed: 2025 (Nature Communications, Volume 16, Article 8633, published September 30, 2025; arXiv preprint 2310.00194 originally October 2023, revised through v5 in 2025)
progenitor: Taylor Webb, Shanka Subhra Mondal, Ida Momennejad (Universite de Montreal, Microsoft Research — I. Momennejad is also affiliated with Microsoft Research NYC)
driving_insight: Modern LLMs like GPT-4 demonstrate competence in individual planning sub-skills (judging action legality, predicting outcomes, evaluating goal progress) when tested in isolation, yet they FAIL when asked to autonomously integrate all these sub-skills into coherent multi-step plans — producing hallucinated paths, getting stuck in loops, and making illegal moves. The brain doesn't solve planning with a single monolithic module either. The prefrontal cortex (PFC) uses specialized subregions that each handle a distinct planning function: aPFC for task decomposition, dlPFC for action selection, ACC for conflict monitoring, and OFC for outcome prediction and evaluation. MAP instantiates this modular architecture with 6 specialized LLM modules, each performing one function. This division of labor prevents the 'cognitive overload' that causes monolithic LLMs to fail on multi-step planning. Ablation studies proved every module is necessary — removing any one causes performance collapse.
decision_style: modular deliberative with limited tree search — the Orchestrator coordinates 5 other modules in a structured multi-step process. The Actor proposes candidate actions, the Monitor filters illegal ones, the Predictor simulates outcomes, the Evaluator scores them, and the Orchestrator selects the best path. This is hierarchical (Orchestrator at top, others are supporting roles) with elements of algorithmic search (bounded-width, bounded-depth lookahead tree using Predictor + Evaluator). Decisions are made through structured deliberation across specialized modules, not through a single model's end-to-end reasoning.
problem_class_targeted: deterministic, well-defined planning problems with known state transitions and clear success criteria. Specifically: (1) Tower of Hanoi (3-4 disks) — classic sequential planning with constraints; (2) Graph traversal / shortest path — navigation in known graphs; (3) Blocks World and Logistics from PlanBench — classical AI planning domains; (4) StrategyQA — multi-hop reasoning. Designed for problems where the action space is enumerable, state transitions are deterministic, and correctness is objectively verifiable. The authors emphasize that MAP targets problems where monolithic LLMs fail despite having the component skills — i.e., the integration bottleneck.
ordered_phases: MAP operates in three nested loops: OUTER LOOP (Plan Generation): (1) TaskDecomposer receives the high-level goal and produces an ordered sequence of subgoals. MIDDLE LOOP (Per-Subgoal Planning): (2) For each subgoal, the Actor proposes candidate next-actions based on the current state; (3) Monitor checks each candidate action for legality — illegal actions are filtered out; (4) Predictor simulates the state resulting from each legal candidate action; (5) Evaluator scores each resulting state for proximity to the current subgoal; (6) Steps 2-5 repeat (bounded lookahead) with the top-k candidates expanded further, forming a limited search tree; (7) Orchestrator selects the best action based on the search results and updates the state. INNER LOOP (Feedback): Monitor can provide feedback to Actor to revise illegal proposals. OUTER LOOP CONTINUATION: (8) Orchestrator checks if the subgoal is achieved — if yes, advance to next subgoal; if all subgoals achieved, task is complete.
feedback_loops: Explicit feedback loops at multiple levels: (1) Monitor-to-Actor: if the Monitor rejects a proposed action as illegal, feedback is sent to the Actor to generate a new proposal — this is a tight corrective loop; (2) Predictor-to-Evaluator: the Evaluator's score of a predicted state feeds into the Orchestrator's decision and also informs whether to expand the search tree further; (3) Orchestrator-to-TaskDecomposer: if after exhaustive search no action achieves the current subgoal, the Orchestrator may trigger re-decomposition (though this is limited in the current implementation). The Monitor-Actor feedback loop is the most critical — without it, illegal action rates spike from <1% to 31%.
gates: Multiple module-level gates: (1) Monitor legality gate — proposed actions that violate rules are blocked; (2) Evaluator quality gate — predicted states are scored, and low-scoring paths are pruned from the search tree; (3) Orchestrator subgoal completion gate — the Orchestrator verifies whether the current state satisfies the subgoal before advancing; (4) Orchestrator task completion gate — all subgoals must be satisfied for task completion. If a gate blocks: the Monitor gate triggers Actor revision; the Evaluator gate causes path pruning; the subgoal gate triggers further action selection. These gates are built into the module interaction protocol, not external checkpoints.
exit_criteria: Success exit: all subgoals from the TaskDecomposer's plan have been achieved, as verified by the Orchestrator. Failure exit: the search tree is exhausted without finding a valid path to the subgoal, or a maximum step limit is reached. For Towers of Hanoi (3 disks): success means all disks moved to the target peg in the minimum number of moves (7). For 4 disks (15 moves), the 24% success rate means 76% of attempts failed to find the optimal path within the search budget.
decomposition_method: hierarchical, goal-driven, single-LLM decomposition via the TaskDecomposer module (inspired by aPFC function). The TaskDecomposer receives the high-level goal and produces a linear sequence of subgoals. For Towers of Hanoi: subgoals correspond to moving the largest disk, then recursively solving the smaller sub-problem. For graph traversal: subgoals are intermediate nodes along the path. This is a TOP-DOWN decomposition: start with the final goal and work backward to identify prerequisite subgoals. The decomposition is NOT recursive — it produces a flat list of subgoals rather than a tree. This is a key difference from RDD's recursive decomposition.
granularity_guidance: Subgoals are defined by the TaskDecomposer at a level that makes each subgoal achievable within the bounded search tree. For Towers of Hanoi (3 disks): typically 2-3 subgoals. For Blocks World: typically 3-5 subgoals. The decomposition is not atomic (unlike MAKER's m=1 per step) — each subgoal may require multiple actions. The granularity is determined by what the Predictor + Evaluator search can effectively handle (the search budget). There is no explicit 'stop decomposition' rule — the TaskDecomposer's output is accepted as-is without further verification.
boundary_rules: Subgoal boundaries are defined by the TaskDecomposer and enforced by the Orchestrator: the Orchestrator switches to the next subgoal only when the current one is verified as achieved. There is no explicit MECE enforcement — subgoals could theoretically overlap or have gaps. The Monitor enforces action-level legality (individual moves must be legal) but does not enforce subgoal-level boundary integrity. State is passed between subgoals via the shared state representation, creating implicit continuity. The boundaries are sequential (subgoal 2 starts only after subgoal 1 is achieved) with no parallel subgoals.
risk_assessment_mechanism: No formal risk assessment. Implicit risk signals: (1) the Evaluator's scores for predicted states — consistently low scores signal that the current subgoal may be unachievable; (2) the Monitor's illegal-action rate — high rejection rates signal that the Actor is struggling with the current state; (3) search tree exhaustion — running out of candidates before finding a valid path signals planning failure. These are diagnostic signals, not a calibrated risk model. The paper does not provide probabilistic risk estimates.
unknown_handling: When the Monitor cannot validate a proposed action (e.g., the state representation is ambiguous), it defaults to REJECTING the action, triggering the Actor to propose alternatives. When the TaskDecomposer faces a novel task, it relies on the LLM's generalization — there is no explicit 'unknown' handling. For out-of-distribution tasks (4-disk Hanoi, not seen in training), performance drops sharply (24% vs. 74% for 3-disk), indicating that the system does not reliably detect when it is operating beyond its competence.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE at the action level (Monitor rejects what it cannot validate) but PERMISSIVE at the planning level (TaskDecomposer proposes a plan even for unfamiliar tasks without confidence calibration). This mixed stance creates a risk: the TaskDecomposer may confidently produce an incorrect decomposition that the action-level modules faithfully attempt to execute, failing only after expending compute.
safety_guardrails: Moderate safety via modular isolation: (1) the Monitor acts as an action-level safety filter — illegal actions are caught before execution; (2) the modular architecture provides natural containment — a hallucination in the Evaluator does not directly affect the Actor's proposals; (3) the Orchestrator provides top-level oversight. However, these are functional safety mechanisms, not security mechanisms — MAP has no defenses against prompt injection, adversarial inputs, or tool misuse. The ablation studies showed that removing the Monitor causes illegal action rates to spike from <1% to 31%, confirming its role as the primary safety filter.
human_decision_points: No human decision points are built into MAP's execution loop. Humans are involved only in: (1) defining the module prompts; (2) setting the search budget (lookahead depth and width); (3) evaluating the final output. The human is OFF the loop during planning. For safety-critical applications, the Orchestrator's decision points (subgoal achievement, final task completion) could be gated on human approval, but this is not implemented in the current system.
override_capability: Humans can override at the input level (change the goal, re-prompt the TaskDecomposer) and the output level (reject the plan). No mid-execution override mechanism exists. Given MAP's architecture, the natural override points would be: after subgoal decomposition (inspect and adjust subgoals) and at each Orchestrator action selection (approve/reject). These are not implemented.
fallback_when_no_human: MAP is designed for autonomous operation. If planning fails (search tree exhausted, Monitor blocks all actions, Orchestrator cannot achieve subgoal), the system fails — there is no graceful degradation or fallback. The paper does not address this scenario as the benchmarks assume solvable problems.
max_problem_size: Tested on: Towers of Hanoi up to 4 disks (4-disk Hanoi: 24% success vs. 5% for best baseline GPT-4 CoT), graph traversal up to 4-step paths (100% for 2-3 steps, 95% for 4 steps), PlanBench suite (Blocks World, Logistics), and StrategyQA reasoning (87.7%). Theoretical limits: (1) the bounded search tree limits planning horizon — deeper problems require exponentially more search; (2) the flat subgoal decomposition limits problem complexity — problems requiring hierarchical sub-sub-goals strain the architecture; (3) the LLM context window — each module's prompt accumulates state, predictions, and evaluations, limiting total planning depth. What breaks: deep planning horizons where the search tree exceeds the budget, and problems needing recursive decomposition.
parallel_execution: Limited parallelism: within the search tree, multiple candidate branches can be expanded in parallel (Predictor + Evaluator can process multiple predicted states concurrently). However, the action selection loop is sequential — one action at a time. Subgoal execution is strictly sequential (subgoal 2 only after subgoal 1). Parallelism is not a design focus — the modular architecture supports it implicitly but the paper does not evaluate parallel execution.
team_scaling_model: The 6 modules are a FIXED team — scaling is not about adding more modules but about the interaction quality between existing ones. Adding redundant modules (e.g., multiple Actors) is not explored. The ablation study shows that all 6 modules are necessary — removing any one causes performance collapse, suggesting that the team size (6) is the minimum effective unit. The model can use smaller, cheaper LLMs (Llama 3 70B) effectively within this modular architecture, making it team-size-efficient in terms of compute cost.
cost_scaling_model: Cost is dominated by the number of LLM calls: each planning step requires calls to Actor, Monitor, Predictor, Evaluator, and Orchestrator. With bounded lookahead of width w and depth d: total calls per step ~ O(w * d) for Predictor/Evaluator pairs + constant calls to Actor, Monitor, TaskDecomposer, and Orchestrator. For tasks requiring t steps: total cost ~ O(t * w * d). The paper emphasizes that smaller models (Llama 3 70B vs. GPT-4) can be used effectively, reducing per-call cost. Compared to monolithic approaches: MAP uses MORE calls per step (due to modularity) but achieves higher success rates, resulting in lower cost-per-successful-plan. No branching factor analysis is provided.
replanning_built_in: Limited replanning: the Monitor-to-Actor feedback loop enables replanning at the action level (propose a different action if the current one is illegal). At the subgoal level: if the search tree is exhausted without achieving the subgoal, the Orchestrator could theoretically request re-decomposition from the TaskDecomposer, but this is not implemented in the paper. The architecture supports replanning structurally (each module can be re-invoked with updated context) but the paper does not explore dynamic replanning.
trigger_mechanism: Action-level trigger: Monitor rejection of an action triggers the Actor to propose an alternative. Subgoal-level trigger: Evaluator scores below threshold could trigger re-decomposition (theoretical, not implemented). Search tree exhaustion triggers failure (no replanning). No environmental change or confidence-drop triggers — MAP operates in static, fully-observable environments where the state only changes through planned actions.
adaptation_cost: Action-level adaptation (re-proposing after Monitor rejection) costs one additional LLM call to the Actor. This is low-cost local adaptation. Subgoal re-decomposition would cost: one TaskDecomposer call + recomputation of the search tree for the new subgoal — moderate cost. No global replanning mechanism exists, so global adaptation cost is undefined.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Action-level recovery: the Monitor filters illegal actions, preventing them from being executed. If the Actor consistently proposes illegal actions, the Monitor's feedback should guide the Actor toward legal proposals (the paper shows this works — illegal actions <1% with Monitor, 31% without). Subgoal-level: no explicit recovery — if a subgoal cannot be achieved, the system fails. No retry with different decomposition, no escalation. The modular architecture provides BLAST RADIUS CONTAINMENT: a hallucination in the Predictor or Evaluator affects only the current search tree, not the overall plan.
output_artifacts: The plan: an ordered sequence of subgoals (from TaskDecomposer) with the action sequences to achieve each subgoal. Supporting artifacts: search tree traces (which candidates were expanded, with Evaluator scores), Monitor validation logs, Predictor state simulations, Orchestrator decisions at each step. These artifacts provide full transparency into the planning process. The paper presents results as: success rates per task, comparison tables against baselines, ablation study results, and per-module performance metrics.
briefing_format: The natural briefing format is the subgoal-action plan: (1) Subgoal 1 -> Actions [a1, a2, ...] -> Subgoal 1 achieved; (2) Subgoal 2 -> Actions [...] -> Subgoal 2 achieved; ... Final goal achieved. This can be accompanied by the search tree visualization showing how each action was selected. For human understanding, the subgoal decomposition is the most compressed format — it shows WHAT the plan does without the search details of HOW each action was chosen.
traceability: Strong traceability: every chosen action traces back through the Evaluator's scores, the Predictor's simulation, and the Monitor's validation. The Orchestrator's decision (which action to take) is documented with the Evaluator scores that justified it. The TaskDecomposer's rationale for the subgoal decomposition is the weakest link — the LLM generates subgoals but does not document WHY those specific subgoals were chosen. This is a black-box step in the traceability chain.
agent_communication_protocol: No external communication protocol — the 6 modules are all LLM calls orchestrated by a single controller. Inter-module communication is through structured prompts (each module receives specific formatted input and produces structured output). There is no message serialization, discovery, or network protocol. The module interaction is best described as a functional pipeline with feedback loops within a single process. If MAP modules were distributed across agents, a simple request-response protocol (JSON over REST) would suffice, as the communication pattern is strictly orchestrated.
learning_memory_architecture: No built-in learning across runs. Each planning problem is solved independently. The module prompts are static few-shot examples. The paper notes that MAP can be integrated with memory mechanisms (e.g., caching successful plans for recurring subgoals) but does not implement this. The brain-inspired architecture suggests that episodic memory (recalling similar past planning problems) could improve performance, but this is left as future work.
latency_bounds: Interactive-to-batch — planning a 3-disk Towers of Hanoi solution takes seconds to minutes depending on the search width and depth. Each planning step involves multiple LLM calls (Actor, Monitor, Predictor, Evaluator, Orchestrator), each with API latency. The bounded search tree controls worst-case latency. Not suitable for hard real-time applications. Under time pressure: reduce search width/depth (trading planning quality for speed). The paper does not provide detailed latency measurements.
evaluation_benchmarking: Evaluated on: (1) Towers of Hanoi (3-disk: 74% MAP vs. 11% GPT-4 CoT; 4-disk out-of-distribution: 24% vs. 5%); (2) Graph traversal/shortest path (2-3 steps: 100%, 4 steps: 95%); (3) PlanBench: Blocks World and Logistics — MAP outperformed all baselines; (4) StrategyQA reasoning: 87.7% (exceeded GPT-4 CoT at 84.7%, approached human ceiling at 87%). Baselines: GPT-4 zero-shot, GPT-4 CoT, Tree of Thoughts (ToT), Multi-Agent Debate (MAD). Model: GPT-4 primarily, with Llama 3 70B for cost-effectiveness experiments. Metrics: success rate (exact path match for planning, exact answer match for QA). Ablation study: removing each module individually to measure its contribution.
mapping_strength: Strong mapping to JORDAN pipeline: the 6 modules map naturally to JORDAN nodes. TaskDecomposer -> plan node (decomposition into subgoals). Actor -> execute/research nodes (proposing actions). Monitor -> risk_assessment node (validating proposed actions). Predictor -> risk_assessment node (simulating outcomes). Evaluator -> synthesize node (scoring progress toward goal). Orchestrator -> approval_gate node (high-level coordination and gating). The Monitor's illegal-action filtering is directly applicable to JORDAN's tool-use safety. The search tree (Predictor + Evaluator) provides a template for JORDAN's multi-hypothesis exploration.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) MAP handles only deterministic, fully-observable planning — JORDAN operates in stochastic, partially-observable environments (code execution outcomes, research results); (2) no tool use — MAP modules reason internally but don't call external APIs, search the web, or execute code; JORDAN agents must act in the world; (3) fixed 6-module architecture — JORDAN may need different module compositions per task type; (4) no learning — MAP doesn't improve with experience, limiting JORDAN's ability to get better over repeated task types; (5) flat subgoal decomposition — cannot handle multi-level task hierarchies that JORDAN's recursive decomposition requires; (6) no error recovery beyond action re-proposal — JORDAN needs global replanning; (7) the PFC-inspired modularity assumes that planning functions cleanly separate, but real-world tasks may have intertwined dependencies that resist clean modular boundaries.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Extend MAP's module set with JORDAN-specific modules: a Research module (web search, document retrieval), a Code Execution module (sandboxed execution with output capture), and a Replan module; (2) Replace MAP's bounded tree search with a more flexible exploration strategy that can handle non-deterministic outcomes (e.g., Monte Carlo tree search with LLM rollouts); (3) Add tool-use capability to the Actor — instead of proposing text actions, propose tool calls with parameters; (4) Implement the Monitor as JORDAN's runtime safety validator — check tool calls before execution; (5) Add persistent memory — cache successful subgoal-action pairs for reuse; (6) Implement hierarchical TaskDecomposer — support recursive decomposition for deeply nested tasks; (7) Use MAP's ablation study methodology to validate that each JORDAN node contributes measurably to overall performance.
validation_context: Peer-reviewed and published in Nature Communications (Volume 16, Article 8633, September 2025) — one of the highest-impact scientific journals (IF 15.7, Q1). Evaluated on 5 benchmarks spanning classical planning (Hanoi, graph traversal, Blocks World, Logistics) and reasoning (StrategyQA). Compared against 4 baselines (GPT-4 zero-shot, CoT, ToT, MAD). Ablation study validated necessity of each module. Authors at Universite de Montreal and Microsoft Research. Code available on GitHub.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Out-of-distribution degradation — 4-disk Hanoi success drops to 24% (from 74% for 3-disk), showing poor generalization to larger problem instances; (2) search tree exhaustion — when the bounded lookahead cannot find a path, the system fails even if a solution exists; (3) subgoal decomposition errors — the TaskDecomposer produces subgoals that are not achievable or are in the wrong order, and the system cannot detect this; (4) without Monitor, illegal action rate spikes to 31% — the architecture is fragile to Monitor failure; (5) the system assumes the Evaluator can accurately score partial progress — when the scoring function is misleading (e.g., short-term gain leads to dead end), the search is misdirected; (6) no detection of task unsolvability — the system exhausts the search tree without recognizing that no solution exists; (7) flat decomposition cannot handle problems requiring hierarchical planning (sub-sub-goals); (8) tested only on English-language prompts.
where_still_used: Published in Nature Communications (September 2025) with code on GitHub. As a recent Nature publication, it is primarily a research artifact with growing academic citations. [uncertain] No known production deployments as of mid-2025. The modular brain-inspired architecture is influential in AI planning research. Microsoft Research (co-author Momennejad's affiliation) may pursue integration with their AI systems. The approach is cited in papers on LLM planning, cognitive AI, and modular agent architectures.
